title: Polpette al Sugo
subtitle: Italian meatballs braised in a simple tomato sauce
description: Italian meatballs — polpette — are quite different from the oversized versions found elsewhere. These are made small, about the size of a walnut, rolled from a mixture of beef mince, breadcrumbs soaked in milk, egg, Parmesan, and parsley, then gently braised in a short tomato sauce until tender. They are served as a second course with bread, or over pasta for a more substantial plate. The technique of soaking the breadcrumbs in milk keeps the meatballs moist throughout the braise.
sourceNotes: Polpette al sugo is a standard of Italian home cooking with roots in both Northern and Southern Italian cucina povera tradition. Pellegrino Artusi's La Scienza in Cucina (1891) contains polpette recipes; the milk-soaked breadcrumb method for moisture appears consistently across Italian cookery writing from the early twentieth century onward.

recipeTools.notes: Wide enough to hold all the meatballs in a single layer for browning, then to braise them in the sauce.

glossaryTerms.definition: A paste of bread soaked in liquid — milk or water — used in meatball and meatloaf mixtures. It holds moisture during cooking, preventing the meatballs from drying out.

ingredients.notes: 15–20% fat — leaner mince produces dry meatballs
Fresh or dried — soaked in 4 tbsp of whole milk for 5 minutes

steps: Soak the breadcrumbs in the milk for 5 minutes until soft. Squeeze out any excess milk.
Combine the beef mince, soaked breadcrumbs, egg, Parmesan, parsley, garlic, salt, and pepper in a bowl. Mix with your hands until just combined — do not overwork or the meatballs will be tough.
Shape into balls about 3 cm across — roughly the size of a walnut. You should get around 24. Roll each one in a little flour.
Heat the olive oil in a wide pan over a medium-high heat. Brown the meatballs in batches, turning gently with a spoon, for 4–5 minutes until golden all over. Remove to a plate.
In the same pan, reduce the heat to medium. Cook the sliced garlic in the olive oil for 1 minute. Add the crushed tomatoes and season with salt. Simmer for 5 minutes.
Return the meatballs to the pan, nestling them into the sauce. Spoon sauce over each one. Cover and simmer gently for 20–25 minutes, turning the meatballs once halfway through.
Taste the sauce and adjust seasoning. Scatter with basil and serve — as they are with bread, or over spaghetti or pappardelle.

content.text: (breadcrumbs soaked in milk) is what keeps these meatballs moist through the braise. Do not skip it and do not substitute with dry breadcrumbs straight from the packet. The other key is to mix the meat mixture gently: overworking compresses the proteins and produces a dense, rubbery result.

Soak the breadcrumbs in the milk for 5 minutes, then squeeze out any excess. Combine {{beef-mince}} of beef mince with the soaked breadcrumbs, egg, {{parmesan}} of Parmesan, parsley, garlic, salt, and pepper. Mix gently with your hands until just combined. Shape into balls about 3 cm across and roll each one in a little flour.
Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide pan over a medium-high heat. Brown the meatballs in batches, turning gently, for 4–5 minutes until golden on all sides. Remove to a plate.

In the same pan, cook the sliced garlic in {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil over a medium heat for 1 minute. Add {{tinned-whole-plum-tomatoes}} of crushed tinned tomatoes and salt. Simmer for 5 minutes. Return the meatballs to the pan, nestling them into the sauce. Cover and simmer gently for 20–25 minutes, turning once halfway through. Taste and adjust seasoning. Scatter with fresh basil and serve.
